ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for fried glass noodles; A00GJ (noodle-based dishes) is the closest defensible category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of fried tunghoon is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one plate as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Tunghoon — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Fried Tunghoon (cr_id F05060062). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glass noodles (mung bean starch / cellophane noodles) have a published GI of approximately 39 (low) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. This value is applied to fried tunghoon as the primary carbohydrate source is glass noodles. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 39 × (12.4 × 2.5) / 100 = 39 × 31.0 / 100 ≈ 12.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 316 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 790 mg sodium, representing about 34% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning, limit portion size to 150–200 g, and pair the dish with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glass noodles have a low GI of approximately 39 due to mung bean starch composition; carbohydrate at 12.4 g/100 g is moderate and sugar is negligible at 0.2 g/100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include fried tunghoon in a controlled portion (150–200 g) as part of a balanced meal, avoiding sweet sauces that raise glycaemic load, and monitoring total carbohydrate intake across the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 316 mg/100 g and stir-frying with oil contribute to cardiovascular risk; fat at 5.4 g/100 g is moderate and saturated fat breakdown is not reported in the HPB source.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request minimal oil and reduced-sodium seasoning, and choose cooking methods that reduce added fat to keep saturated fat and sodium within heart-healthy targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-heat stir-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); dish lacks omega-3 fatty acids or significant polyphenols to provide anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume fried tunghoon sparingly and consider adding anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger or turmeric, or substituting with a steamed or boiled glass noodle preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried tunghoon (炒冬粉) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of glass noodles (also called cellophane noodles or bean thread noodles, made from mung bean starch) stir-fried at high heat with prawns, dried black mushrooms, and chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage), seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The dish has a light, slightly chewy texture with a savoury umami flavour profile. Glass noodles are translucent when cooked and absorb the seasoning well, giving the dish its characteristic glossy appearance. Nutritionally, fried tunghoon provides 114 kcal per 100 g, with moderate carbohydrate (12.4 g), moderate fat (5.4 g), and a useful protein contribution (3.9 g) from the prawns. Dietary fibre at 2.5 g/100 g comes from the vegetables and mushrooms. The dish has a low glycaemic index (approximately 39) due to the mung bean starch base, making it a relatively glucose-friendly noodle option compared to wheat-based noodles or white rice. Sodium is borderline at 316 mg/100 g, primarily from soy sauce and oyster sauce, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians due to the prawn and oyster sauce content. It is commonly served as a main course at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops, and is suitable for lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: Is fried tunghoon suitable for diabetics?
What are glass noodles made of?
Can I eat fried tunghoon if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F05060062) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the analogous glass noodles entry in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the closest approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific fried tunghoon preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F05060062 as of 2026-04-16. The input cultural classification listed 'western' as primary cuisine and 'vegetarian' as a dietary tag; both have been corrected — this is a Singaporean hawker dish containing prawn and oyster sauce and is therefore not vegetarian. The dietary_tags from the input have been overridden accordingly. GI value of 39 is an analogous estimate based on glass noodles (mung bean starch) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for the composite fried tunghoon dish was found in approved sources.
